stimulus: Antigone is a circa 441 BCE play by Sophocles, translated in 1888 by R.C. Jebb. The title character, who is determined to bury her dishonored brother in defiance of King Creon’s edict, is aware of the danger she courts but believes her course to be justified: ______blank
stem: Which quotation from Antigone most effectively illustrates the claim?

answerOptions.content: “O tomb, O bridal chamber, O thou deep-dug home, that art ever to keep watch upon me, whither I go to find mine own.”
“Send word, my sister, send word at once to all our kin in the city, that they may know how I am dealt with by those who rule.”

“I will not be guilty of betraying my brother to the dogs and the open sky; I never doubted the peril, but, since the cause is sacred to the gods, no edict of a mortal hand shall sway me from this purpose.”
“Lead me, then, lead me to that bridal chamber beneath the earth—there I shall make my dwelling, and there I shall lie down to rest.”

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it most effectively illustrates the claim that Antigone is aware of the danger she courts but believes her course to be justified. She names the peril (“I never doubted the peril”) and asserts the justification (“since the cause is sacred to the gods, no edict of a mortal hand shall sway me from this purpose”), exhibiting both the awareness and the justification central to the claim.

Choice A is incorrect because, although it evokes the consequences awaiting her, it neither expresses awareness of present danger as such nor articulates a justification for her course of action. Choice B is incorrect because it is an instruction to her sister, not a statement about Antigone’s own awareness and justification. Choice D is incorrect because it dwells on her impending fate as a destination, not on the awareness-and-justification structure the claim requires.
